title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
text:
Last week’s recap
We covered the multiple testing problem

Running multiple tests can lead to too many false positives
Two methods for addressing the problem

Bonferroni, conservative, maybe too conservative
False Discovery Rate (FDR), preferable when expecting many true signals, such as in GWAS analyses

title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
text:
Learning Objectives

Understand how the genotype matrix encodes population history
Recognize how population structure can bias GWAS results
Learn how PCA / SVD decomposes the genotype matrix to reveal ancestry
Apply principal components as covariates to correct for stratification
Review matrix algebra foundations underlying regression and PCA

title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
text:
Genotype Matrix: A Treasure Trove

Entries: 0 / 1 / 2 = copies of minor allele (n individuals × M SNPs)

The genotype matrix harbors an amazing wealth of information that can be uncovered with various statistical techniques.

title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
text:
Principal Components Reveal Demographic History

Novembre et al., Nature 456, 98–101 (2008)

This is probably one of the most referenced figures in genetics talks. The first two principal components of the genotype matrix of ~1,500 European individuals are shown. When each individual is colored by the country of origin, the map of Europe emerges — a remarkable consequence of genetic similarity tied to geographic proximity.

title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
text:
PCA via SVD

\[\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{D}\mathbf{V}^\top = \sum_k d_k \, \mathbf{u}_k \mathbf{v}_k^\top\]

The SVD factorization decomposes the genotype matrix into orthogonal rank-1 components ordered by variance explained. The columns of U (left singular vectors) are the principal components — coordinates of each individual in the low-dimensional ancestry space. The singular values d_k indicate how much variance each component explains.

title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
section: Could Population Structure Bias GWAS Results?
text:
Could Population Structure Bias GWAS Results?


When cases and controls are drawn from populations with different allele frequencies, a variant that merely tracks ancestry will appear associated with the phenotype. In this example, MAF = 40% in cases vs 35% in controls — a spurious but statistically significant difference driven entirely by population composition, not biology.

title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
section: How to Correct for Population Structure?
text:
How to Correct for Population Structure?

Genomic control (Devlin & Roeder 1999) Divide all χ² by inflation factor λ — simple, but blunt
Infer latent sub-populations (Pritchard et al. 2000) STRUCTURE / ADMIXTURE → run GWAS within each group and meta-analyze
PCs as covariates (Patterson 2006; Price et al. 2010) ← most common today Add top PCs to regression model
Mixed effects modeling (EMMAX, Kang et al. 2010) Kinship / GRM as random effect — gold standard, computationally intensive


If we know the subpopulations, we can run the GWAS within each and meta-analyze. Even if unknown a priori, PCA can identify discrete clusters. The most common approach is using PCs as covariates. Mixed effects modeling handles subtle structure and relatedness but is computationally expensive.

title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
text:
HapMap Project


The International HapMap project recruited individuals who consented to have their genotypes publicly available, with the goal of mapping haplotypes of the human genome.

title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
text:
1000 Genomes Project

Auton et al., Nature 526, 68–74 (2015)

The HapMap project evolved into the 1000 Genomes Project, which aimed to sequence individuals from around the globe. As of 2020, these resources are still heavily used to understand human genetic diversity.

title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
text:
HapMap Phase 3 — Sample Sizes


We can check the number of individuals in each HapMap 3 population using R. Code from the course notebook: https://hakyimlab.github.io/hgen471/L6-population-structure.html

title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
section: Population Structure in HapMap (PC1 vs PC2)
text:
Population Structure in HapMap (PC1 vs PC2)


PC1 separates the African populations (circles) from the European (squares), Asian (triangles), Hispanic (+), and South Asian (crossed square). PC2 further separates European from East Asian clusters. Each cluster corresponds to a geographic/ancestral group.

title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
text:
PCA in UK Biobank

Special methods (flashPCA, BOLT-LMM) required for 500,000+ individuals.

Special methods had to be developed to be able to compute principal components given the sheer size of the UK Biobank data with 500,000 individuals.

title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
text:
Growth Phenotype Differs by Population

Im et al., PLoS Genetics (2012)

Proliferation rates of lymphoblastoid cell lines differ significantly across HapMap populations. Because SNPs also differ in frequency across populations, any SNP correlated with population label will appear associated with growth — a false positive driven by confounding.

title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
text:
Regression: Growth ~ Population


Linear regression of growth phenotype on population yields highly significant differences in mean growth between populations. ASW is the reference (R orders factors alphabetically). This confirms population is a strong confounder that must be accounted for in GWAS.

title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
text:
P-value Histogram: Inflation

igrowth.assoc <- read.table("output/igrowth.assoc.linear", header=T, as.is=T)
hist(igrowth.assoc$P)
J-shaped histogram → inflation (flat = no signal, J-shape = confounding or polygenic)

A flat p-value histogram would indicate no inflation. The J-shape means most variants have smaller-than-expected p-values due to population differences, not biology. We have 20,649 variants on chr 22.

title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
text:
Manhattan Plot: Chr 22

# Bonferroni threshold for 20,649 tests
-log10(0.05 / 20649)  # [1] 5.616

Manhattan plot shows no clear genome-wide significant hit, but there are many variants narrowly below the Bonferroni threshold — a red flag for population stratification inflating test statistics across the entire chromosome.

title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
text:
QQ Plot: Widespread Inflation

qqunif(igrowth.assoc$P)
Departure from identity line at all quantiles → population structure, not true signal

In a well-behaved GWAS, variants follow the identity line (representing true nulls) and depart only for a small set of true associations at the top right. Here the departure begins immediately, indicating that most variants are inflated. This is characteristic of population structure confounding rather than a highly polygenic trait.

title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
text:
Growth GWAS Adjusted with PCs

system(glue("plink --bfile hapmapch22 --linear --pheno igrowth.pheno
  --pheno-name growth --covar pca.eigenvec --covar-number 1-4
  --maf 0.05 --out output/igrowth-adjPC"))

After adjusting for 4 PCs, the p-value distribution is nearly uniform — inflation is corrected. How many PCs to use depends on the application and sample size. UK Biobank analyses typically use 10–20. Always do sensitivity analysis. Do NOT choose the number of PCs that maximizes significance for a cherry-picked variant — that is p-hacking.

title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
text:
Matrix Operations
Scalar multiplication:
\[2 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 8 & -3 \\ 4 & -2 & 5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 16 & -6 \\ 8 & -4 & 10 \end{bmatrix}\]
Transposition:
\[\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 0 & -6 & 7 \end{bmatrix}^{\!\top} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 2 & -6 \\ 3 & 7 \end{bmatrix}\]

title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
text:
Matrix Multiplication

\[(A \cdot B)_{ij} = \sum_{k} A_{ik}\, B_{kj}\]
Order matters: \(AB \neq BA\) in general

title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
text:
Linear System in Matrix Form
\[a_{1,1}x_1 + \cdots + a_{1,n}x_n = b_1\] \[\vdots\] \[a_{m,1}x_1 + \cdots + a_{m,n}x_n = b_m\]
Written compactly: \(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}\)

title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
text:
Derive Linear Regression Solution
\[Y = X\beta + \varepsilon\]
Multiply both sides on the left by \(X^\top\):
\[X^\top Y = X^\top X\,\beta + X^\top\varepsilon\]
Setting \(X^\top\varepsilon = 0\) and solving:
\[\boxed{\hat{\beta} = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top Y}\]

Let’s assume we have demeaned and divided by standard deviation both Y and X. Check dimensions: if X is n×p and Y is n×1, then β is p×1. The OLS solution minimizes the sum of squared residuals.

title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
section: PC Plot of Vanderbilt Patients Across Decades
text:
PC Plot of Vanderbilt Patients Across Decades
Nancy Cox, 2017 ASHG Presidential Address (20:04 – 24:12):

Video excerpt (UChicago Box)
Full talk (YouTube)

PC plot of BioVU patients colored by decade of birth → dramatic shifts in ancestry composition over time

title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
text:
SVD Resources
Daniella Witten’s SVD Tweetorial (24 tweets) twitter.com/WomenInStat/status/1285611207530098688
\[\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{D}\mathbf{V}^\top\]

U columns = PC scores (individual coordinates)
V columns = loadings (SNP weights)
D diagonal = singular values (∝ variance explained)

title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
text:
Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium
Under random mating (paternal and maternal alleles independent):
If \(p\) = minor allele frequency:
\[P(AA) = p^2 \qquad P(AC) = 2p(1-p) \qquad P(CC) = (1-p)^2\]
Test: Chi-square comparing observed vs expected counts:
\[\sum \frac{(O - E)^2}{E} \sim \chi^2_{\text{df}=(r-1)(c-1)}\]
Departures → genotyping error, population structure, or selection

title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
text:
Types of Heritability
\[Y = G + E \qquad Y = A_G + NA_G + E\]







Heritability
Definition




Broad-sense \(H^2\)
\(\mathrm{Var}(G)/\mathrm{Var}(Y)\)


Narrow-sense \(h^2\)
\(\mathrm{Var}(A_G)/\mathrm{Var}(Y)\)


Chip/SNP \(h^2_\text{SNP}\)
Additive component captured by genotyped variants



Additive model: \[Y = \sum_{k=1}^{M} X_k \beta_k + E\]

title: Principal Component Analysis and Population Structure
text:
Summary







Concept
Key point




Genotype matrix
Encodes ancestry; PCA reveals population structure


Novembre 2008
PC1/PC2 of Europeans reproduces geography


Population stratification
Confounds GWAS → spurious associations


Genomic control
Inflation correction via λ — blunt


PCs as covariates
Most widely used; sensitivity-test # of PCs


Mixed models
Best for subtle structure & relatedness


SVD
\(X = UDV^\top\); U columns = PC scores


HWE
Chi-square test; departures flag errors/selection


\(h^2\)
Narrow-sense = additive genetic variance fraction

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
Hae Kyung Im, PhD
March 31, 2025

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
Learning Objectives

GWAS of cancer susceptibility
Learn about QC steps in GWAS
UK Biobank GWAS

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
GWAS


Today we will talk about the discoveries made by GWAS of cancer risk, examples of the insights that were gleaned from them, and challenges to turn discoveries into biological understanding. We will use the UK Biobank as a case study to learn how GWAS is performed and the importance of QC.

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
Manhattan Plot

GWAS significant threshold: 5×10⁻⁸
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2865585/

Manhattan plots are used to visualize GWAS results. Points above −log₁₀(5×10⁻⁸) are called GWAS significant.
“A Manhattan plot (−log10[P] genome-wide association plot) of a genome-wide association study on systolic blood pressure in 29,136 individuals in CHARGE. The genome-wide significance level is set at 5 × 10−8 and plotted as the dotted line. Any SNP within a region of 5 Mb containing a SNP reaching the genome-wide significance threshold is colored in green. The most significant SNP is colored in red.”
Ehret, Genome-Wide Association Studies: Contribution of Genomics to Understanding Blood Pressure and Essential Hypertension, 2011.

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
QQPlot



A Q-Q plot is a useful tool to present GWAS results and check for potential issues
X-axis: expected −log(P) under the null (no association)
Y-axis: observed −log(P)
Dots above the 45° line deserve a closer look
Can also be used to check for population stratification





In addition to the Manhattan plot, QQ plots are a useful visualization of GWAS results to detect possible issues. This compares the observed distribution of p-values with the expected distribution under the null hypothesis.
Under the null, p-values are distributed uniformly. In typical well-behaved GWAS, most points should line up at the identity line and a few at the right end depart from the identity line — those are the true associations.

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
GWAS Cancer Risk



Breast
Prostate
Lung
Colorectal
Pancreatic
Gastric
Renal
Bladder
Glioma
Ovarian
Many more…


430 associations in 262 distinct loci @2017 and counting
Homework: read Sud, A., Kinnersley, B. & Houlston, R. Genome-wide association studies of cancer: current insights and future perspectives. Nat Rev Cancer 17, 692–704 (2017).


Many GWAS have been performed over the last two decades. The number of discoveries keeps growing as more samples are included. As of 2017, 430 loci associated with various cancers were reported.

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
section: Sample Size & Genetic Architecture Drive Discoveries
text:
Sample Size & Genetic Architecture Drive Discoveries


The number of loci that can be identified depends on the sample size and the genetic architecture of the cancer. Most known loci today correspond to breast and prostate cancers, because of the very large sample size. As of 2020, there were 170 breast cancer loci; at 2021, 269 prostate cancer loci.
The genetic architecture — heritability and effect sizes — also determines the GWAS yield. For example, CLL, which is highly heritable with large-effect loci, has 43 loci with ~24K individuals. Whereas lung cancer has only 18 loci even with 86K samples, reflecting the larger role of environmental factors.

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
section: GWAS Loci Provide Insights into Cancer Biology
text:
GWAS Loci Provide Insights into Cancer Biology

In B cell tumors:

B cell developmental and immune response genes
IKZF1, CEBPE, IRF4, IRF8, GATA3 and ARID5B

Testicular cancer:

Developmental transcriptional regulation
Microtubule and chromosomal assembly
KIT-ERK signaling pathway



Some of the discoveries provide insights into the biology of cancers. In B cell tumors, significant loci were in the vicinity of B cell developmental and immune response genes such as IKZF1, CEBPE, IRF4, IRF8, GATA3 and ARID5B.
In testicular cancer, GWAS loci implicated developmental transcriptional regulation, microtubule and chromosomal assembly, and the KIT-ERK signaling pathway.

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
Gene-Environment Interaction

CHRNA3-5 nicotine addiction locus associated with lung cancer
NAT2 in bladder cancer, modifies effect of smoking
Skin pigmentation loci are associated with skin cancer


GWAS loci have recapitulated what we know about environmental factors driving cancer risk. The nicotine addiction locus was associated with lung cancer risk, implicating smoking; NAT2 associated with bladder cancer also implicates smoking; skin pigmentation loci association with skin cancer shows the role of sun exposure.

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
Lung Cancer GWAS


Manhattan plots of lung cancer risk overall and by histological subtype. From: Large-scale association analysis identifies new lung cancer susceptibility loci and heterogeneity in genetic susceptibility across histological subtypes.

Lung cancer risk overall: 29,266 cases and 56,450 controls.
Adenocarcinoma: 11,273 cases and 55,483 controls.
Squamous cell carcinoma: 7,426 cases and 55,627 controls.

Black = previously known loci; red = new loci. The red horizontal line denotes P = 5 × 10⁻⁸.

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
section: Vast Majority of Loci Still Need Mechanistic Explanation
text:
Vast Majority of Loci Still Need Mechanistic Explanation

Most loci are located outside of coding regions
Target gene is unknown
Effect sizes are small


Most GWAS loci do not have an obvious biological explanation. Even the acting gene is not well known because most loci are in non-coding regions — they have a regulatory role but which gene they regulate is not completely clear. The nearest gene is the suspect but the causal gene may be several hundred kilobases away. The small effect sizes also make the explanation difficult.

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
GWAS vs. Candidate Gene Studies


GWAS:

Agnostic survey
Successfully discovered many cancer susceptibility loci


Candidate gene studies:

Led to underpowered studies
Mostly discouraged today



Avoid candidate gene studies

https://www.nature.com/articles/s41386-019-0389-5

Beware of candidate gene studies. There were way too many that did not replicate.

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
UK Biobank

UK Biobank started recruitment of participants in 2006, even before the WTCCC GWAS study publication — a testament of the forward-looking vision of the proponents. This is a picture of the huge freezer with automated handling of the biological samples, giving a sense of the scale of the biobank.
Their liberal data sharing policies made it possible for thousands of investigators to examine this data, yielding more than 1000 publications to date (as of beginning 2020).
Biological Samples in a Storage Freezer at the UK Biobank — Nancy Cox, UK Biobank shares the promise of big data, 2018, Nature.

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
section: UK Biobank: Genotype and Phenotype Data
text:
UK Biobank: Genotype and Phenotype Data





Large prospective population-based cohort study
Over 500,000 participants enrolled
Participants aged 40–69, between 2006 and 2010
Deeply phenotyped:

Questionnaires
Physical & biological measurements
Electronic health records (EHR)
Images, accelerometer measurements (subset)

Genotype data (488K)



The depth of the phenotypes is astonishing. Electronic health records have been linked with the participants. The single-payer health care system in the UK made it possible to get this amount of information in a relatively uniform fashion.

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
UK Biobank: Ancestries

White 94.23% (88.26% British)
Asian 1.92%
Black 1.57%
Chinese 0.31%
Mixed 0.58%
Other 1.38%

Bycroft et al. (2018). The UK Biobank resource with deep phenotyping and genomic data. Nature.

The biobank is mostly composed of White British individuals, with a small portion of Asian (1.92%), Black (1.57%), Chinese (0.31%), and others.

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
UK Biobank: Self-Reported Cancers


Self-reported cancers in the UK Biobank. 53K cancer diagnoses were reported by the participants at the time of recruitment (2006–2010).
https://biobank.ndph.ox.ac.uk/showcase/field.cgi?id=20001

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
UK Biobank: Cancer Registry


Thanks to the national cancer registry in the UK, the cancer diagnosis information continues to accrue. In 2019 there were 84,722 participants with a cancer diagnosis (ICD10), which went up to 119,346 by 3/25/2024.

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
UK Biobank: Genotyping Chips



UK BiLEVE (UK Biobank Lung Exam Variant Evaluation)

N ~ 50K
UK BiLEVE Axiom Array by Affymetrix (807K markers)

UK Biobank (remaining)

N ~ 438K
UK Biobank Axiom Array by Applied Biosystems






Two genotyping chips were used. Initially the UK BiLEVE Axiom Array with 807K markers was used for about 50K participants. The remaining 90% of the participants were genotyped with the newer UK Biobank Axiom Array, specifically designed for this study.

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
UK Biobank Axiom Array Content


The UK Biobank Axiom Array contained 805K markers chosen with several criteria:

High coverage of common variation to be used as a scaffold for genotype imputation (~630K)
Rare coding variants (~125K) — rare variation must be relevant for health and disease
eQTLs — variants known to regulate gene expression
Markers within genomic regions of interest (~47K)
Markers relevant to specific phenotypes (~45K)
Higher coverage of the complex HLA region and immune-implicated variants
Copy number markers

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
Why Is QC Important?

QC is the least glamorous part of research and analysis. So why should we care about QC? To avoid huge pitfalls and draw spurious conclusions.
General rule: if something sounds too good to be true, it is highly likely it is not true. Before making big claims, make sure your QC is super solid. Think of every possible confounder that could lead to the “interesting” results.
In one notorious paper, the authors had found many SNPs associated with being a centenarian — they thought they had found the “longevity genes.”

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
Why Is QC Important?

They found out that some genotyping chips were faulty — and those chips disproportionately affected centenarians vs. controls.

Faulty chips were confounded with case status → false positives
Original claim of 77% accuracy to predict longevity could not be supported with QC’d data




How could this have been detected before publication? What QC check would catch it?


After the publication, they realized that 10% of the centenarians had been genotyped on faulty chips. If they had checked for a batch/chip effect before analysis — comparing the distribution of any variable between batches — they would have caught the confounding between chip and longevity status.

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
section: We Want to Avoid This Kind of Publicity
text:
We Want to Avoid This Kind of Publicity
Retracted paper: claimed 77% accuracy to predict longevity from SNPs

Root cause: faulty chips disproportionately used on cases
Caught after publication

https://www.nytimes.com/2011/07/23/science/23retract.html

You really don’t want to appear in the NY Times as the scientist who had to retract a paper because of faulty QC.

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
GWAS QC Workflow


Here is a summary of the standard workflow for QC in GWAS. It covers three parallel tracks: sample quality (sex inconsistency checks, sample relatedness, population stratification, genotyping call rate), batch effect analysis (average call rate and MAF, low-MAF SNP exclusion, plate vs. all comparison), and marker quality (call rate, HapMap concordance, minor allele frequency, Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium).

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
Marker-Based QC



Test
Fraction affected
Avg SNPs failed/batch




Affymetrix cluster QC
0.00140
1109 (699)


1. Batch effect
0.000249
197 (86)


2. Plate effect
0.000358
284 (266)


3. Departure from HWE
0.000723
572 (77)


4. Sex effect
0.0000569
45 (5)


5. Array effect
0.00683
5417


6. Discordance across controls
0.000796
622 and 632


Total
0.00971
7704 (721)



Clare Bycroft et al., Nature 2018

The UKB QC pipeline was designed to accommodate a large-scale, ethnically diverse dataset genotyped in 106 batches using two slightly different arrays.
TL;DR: less than 1% of markers were removed due to QC problems.
Markers were filtered by: Affymetrix cluster QC (failure in genotype calling), batch effects (~106 batches of ~5000 individuals), plate effects (DNA in 96-well plates), Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium departure, sex effects, and array effects.

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
Minor Allele Frequencies of 805K Markers


Here is the distribution of minor allele frequencies in the UKB.
About 130K markers had allele frequencies below 1%. Half of rare variants were found in at least 1000 individuals. 20K were present in fewer than 10 participants (given rarity, there probably weren’t two copies of these rare alleles in one individual).

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
QC: Success/Failure Rates by MAF


Most common variants — more than 95% (blue, rightmost bar) — passed QC in all batches. Quality was overall pretty good even for low frequency variants: over 80% of the very rare variants passed QC in all batches (leftmost bar above 0).

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
Missing Rates for Markers


Missing rates were low, with most of the mass under 0.01.
Pink bar corresponds to markers exclusively present in the old array (UKBiLEVE), meaning ~90% of people did not have a value for those. Blue corresponds to markers only available in the new array, so ~10% of participants lacked those genotypes. This explains the two peaks at ~0.1 and ~0.9.

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
section: Comparison of Allele Frequency with ExAC
text:
Comparison of Allele Frequency with ExAC


Allele frequencies of all UKB variants were compared to population frequencies available from the ExAC consortium. The markers lie near the identity line, providing reassurance that the genotyping was reliable.

Some variants are high frequency in ExAC but not found in UKB
Very few the other way around (high frequency in UKB but not observed in ExAC)

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
ExAC Aggregation Consortium → gnomAD

https://gnomad.broadinstitute.org/

The ExAC database, now renamed gnomAD, is a huge publicly available resource summarizing a very large number of whole-exome and whole-genome sequenced samples. This resource is critical to evaluate the pathogenicity of rare variants.
If a variant appears in relatively high numbers in gnomAD, we can safely assume that reasonably healthy life is possible with the mutation. When first released, many variants previously catalogued as highly pathogenic ended up being reclassified as variants of uncertain significance (VUS).

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
gnomAD Browser


A snapshot of the gnomAD webpage search for the FTO gene. The browser shows constraint metrics (Z scores for synonymous, missense, and pLoF variants) and coverage tracks across the gene body.

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
Intensity Plots for Genotype Calling


Here are examples of the intensity plots used for genotype calling. By plotting the signal strength vs. the contrast of the intensities (log ratio of A and B alleles), we can visualize distinct clusters corresponding to the three genotypes (AA, AB, BB) and no-calls (×). These clusters are used for genotype calling.

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
Sex Mismatches and Chromosome Aneuploidy


Average log intensities (normalized) of Y chromosome and X chromosome markers can help infer the sex of participants. The green cluster has “deficient” Y chromosome markers whereas the pink cluster shows X chromosome marker deficiency. XXY is centered at 0 due to the choice of normalization.
“L2R are computed (by Affymetrix) for each sample at each marker, and is the sum of the A and B allele intensities for the marker, normalized by the median intensity of that marker in individuals assumed to represent the normal copy number state at that site.”

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
Sex-Specific Intensities in Smaller Sample

Turner, S., Armstrong, L. L., et al. (2011). Quality control procedures for genome-wide association studies. Current Protocols in Human Genetics.

Much fewer cases of aneuploidy (missing or extra chromosomes) are seen in smaller samples. The XY males cluster (blue triangles) separate clearly from XX females (pink circles), with XXY karyotype individuals in the intermediate region.

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
Missing Rates for Samples


Missing rates are different for the first 50K individuals and the remaining 438K, driven by the difference in array. The first 50K were genotyped with the UKBiLEVE Axiom Array (higher missing rates, pink), whereas the remaining 438K individuals were genotyped with the UK Biobank Axiom Array (lower missing rates, blue).

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
QC: Array Effect


Comparison of genotype cluster plots across two arrays (UKBiLEVE and UKBiobank Axiom Arrays). This marker has an outlier for a UKBiLEVE batch that is not present in the newer array — the orange cluster in the UKBiLEVE batch is shifted relative to the UKBiobank batches. Such markers fail the array effect QC step.

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
QC: Batch Effect (109 batches)


Batch b001 (red box) shows a clear outlier cluster — a genotype cluster that is not present in batches b002, b003, b004. This marker fails the batch effect QC step because the genotype calls are inconsistent across batches, likely due to chip or reagent variability.

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
QC: Sex Effect


Data points cluster by sex (males vs. females) rather than by genotype (AA, AB, BB). This indicates that the marker is on a sex chromosome and genotype calling does not account for sex — the clusters are unreliable. Such markers are excluded.

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium
Under random mating, variant alleles from father & mother are independent.
Given MAF \(p\), expected genotype frequencies:
\[aa: p^2 \qquad aA: 2p(1-p) \qquad AA: (1-p)^2\]
Significant departure from HWE → possible genotyping error

Example of a marker that does not pass the HWE test: the observed heterozygote frequency (AB) is much higher or lower than expected under random mating. This can indicate a genotyping problem (e.g., heterozygote excess due to poor cluster separation).

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
QC: Plate Effect


Right figure shows plates with different colors. The pink plate data clusters on its own, separate from other plates, messing up the genotype calling. Participants’ DNA on the same plate can show systematic differences if there were reagent batch effects or handling differences during sample preparation.

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
section: UK Biobank vs. GIANT: Height GWAS
text:
UK Biobank vs. GIANT: Height GWAS


GWAS results for height in UKB are compared to an independent GWAS of height from the GIANT consortium. UKB p-values are more significant than GIANT’s p-values due to the larger sample size in UKB as well as less heterogeneity (UKB is primarily White British, while GIANT is a meta-analysis of many diverse cohorts).

title: Lecture 3: GWAS, QC & UK Biobank
text:
References

Sud, A., Kinnersley, B. & Houlston, R. Genome-wide association studies of cancer: current insights and future perspectives. Nat Rev Cancer 17, 692–704 (2017). https://doi.org/10.1038/nrc.2017.82
UK Biobank Imputed Genotype Data Release
Bycroft, C., Freeman, C., Petkova, D., et al. (2018). The UK Biobank resource with deep phenotyping and genomic data. Nature, 562(7726). http://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-018-0579-z
S. Turner et al. (2011). Quality control procedures for genome-wide association studies. Current Protocols in Human Genetics.
Anderson et al. (2010). Data quality control in genetic case-control association studies. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3025522/

title: Lecture 4: Multiple Testing Correction
text:
Last Time: GWAS & QC
GWAS in the UK Biobank — and why quality control matters before any analysis.
Key QC checks to always perform:

Batch effects — systematic differences when samples are genotyped on different platforms, chips, or at different centers
Missingness

SNPs with too many missing calls across individuals
Individuals with too many missing SNPs

Sex mismatch — does inferred genetic sex match reported sex?
Minor allele frequencies — compare observed MAFs to expected values from a reference panel (e.g., gnomAD)







Note


We went through the UKB QC examples quickly last time — refer back to the L3 slides for the full details.

title: Lecture 4: Multiple Testing Correction
text:
The Perils of Multiple Testing


Let’s look at this cartoon case study, which happens more often than expected. When you test many hypotheses, some will appear significant purely by chance.

title: Lecture 4: Multiple Testing Correction
text:
The Perils of Multiple Testing

What happened? Is the conclusion sensible?
https://xkcd.com/882/

The xkcd comic illustrates the problem perfectly. When you test 20 colors of jelly beans for a link to acne at a 0.05 significance level, you expect 1 false positive just by chance. “Green jelly beans linked to acne” — not because of any real effect, but because of multiple testing.

title: Lecture 4: Multiple Testing Correction
section: Probability of False Positives Accumulates Fast
text:
Probability of False Positives Accumulates Fast
What is the probability of not rejecting the null (staying safe from false positives) when testing at α = 0.05?



Number of tests
P(no false positive)




1
(1 − 0.05)^1 = 0.95


2
(1 − 0.05)^2 = 0.90


100
(1 − 0.05)^100 = 0.0059




Homework: Plot P(no false positive) as a function of the number of tests m.


How do we solve this problem?


With just 100 independent tests at α = 0.05, the probability that you will make at least one false positive is 1 − 0.0059 = 99.4%. Multiple testing rapidly destroys the meaning of a nominal significance level.

title: Lecture 4: Multiple Testing Correction
text:
Bonferroni Correction
\[\alpha_{\text{Bonferroni}} = \frac{0.05}{\text{number of tests}}\]
When in doubt: divide 0.05 by the number of tests and use this more stringent cutoff.

Q: What could be the downside of using this very stringent threshold?



Bonferroni is simple and conservative. The downside: if many of the null hypotheses are actually false (i.e., many true signals exist), Bonferroni will miss them — it sacrifices power to control false positives very tightly.

title: Lecture 4: Multiple Testing Correction
text:
Genome-Wide Significance Level
\[5 \times 10^{-8}\]
Q: If this were a Bonferroni threshold, how many tests are we correcting for?
Q: Is the number of tests = the number of SNPs tested in a GWAS?

5 × 10⁻⁸ corresponds to Bonferroni correction for ~1 million independent tests (0.05 / 1,000,000 = 5 × 10⁻⁸). However, the actual number of SNPs tested is typically much larger (e.g., 5–10 million). The key insight is that SNPs are correlated due to linkage disequilibrium, so the effective number of independent tests is around 1 million, not the raw SNP count.

title: Lecture 4: Multiple Testing Correction
section: What Does the P-value Distribution Look Like under the Null?
text:
What Does the P-value Distribution Look Like under the Null?






Q: Can you rule out some of these distributions based on the definition of a p-value?

Under the null hypothesis, p-values are uniformly distributed between 0 and 1. So a histogram that is flat is consistent with the null. A distribution that is enriched near 0 suggests true signals are present. A distribution enriched near 1 would be unusual and could indicate issues such as opposite-direction effects being tested, or model misspecification.

title: Lecture 4: Multiple Testing Correction
text:
Simulations: Setup
To develop intuition for how p-values behave under the null and alternatives, we run simulations.
\[Y_{\text{null}} = X \cdot 0 + \epsilon' \quad \text{(no true effect)}\]
\[Y_{\text{alt}} = X \cdot \beta + \epsilon \quad \text{(true genetic effect)}\]

nsamp  = 100               # sample size
beta   = 2                 # effect size
h2     = 0.1               # heritability (proportion of variance explained by X)
sig2X  = h2
sig2epsi = (1 - sig2X) * beta^2
sigX   = sqrt(sig2X)
sigepsi = sqrt(sig2epsi)
https://bios25328.hakyimlab.org/post/2022/04/06/multiple-testing-2022/

We define two scenarios: Ynull where X has zero effect on Y, and Yalt where X affects Y with effect size β. We set heritability h² = 0.1 so that 10% of the variance in Y is explained by X.
X is the genotype (simulated from a normal distribution with sd = sigX). Ynull is generated with no effect: Ynull = rnorm(nsamp, mean=0, sd=sigepsi) Yalt is generated with a true effect: Yalt = X * beta + rnorm(nsamp, mean=0, sd=sigepsi)

title: Lecture 4: Multiple Testing Correction
section: Plot Y vs. X Under the Alternative
text:
Plot Y vs. X Under the Alternative

https://bios25328.hakyimlab.org/post/2022/04/06/multiple-testing-2022/

Under the alternative hypothesis, we can see a clear positive trend — as X (genotype dosage) increases, Y increases on average. The slope is the estimated effect size β.

title: Lecture 4: Multiple Testing Correction
section: Plot Y vs. X Under the Null
text:
Plot Y vs. X Under the Null

https://bios25328.hakyimlab.org/post/2022/04/06/multiple-testing-2022/

Under the null hypothesis, there is no trend — the regression line is essentially flat, and any apparent slope is noise. Running a regression here should yield a p-value that is not particularly small.

title: Lecture 4: Multiple Testing Correction
section: Getting a P-value from Linear Regression
text:
Getting a P-value from Linear Regression
Run lm() and extract the p-value from the summary() output:
fit <- lm(Y ~ X)
pval <- summary(fit)$coefficients["X", "Pr(>|t|)"]

summary(fit)$coefficients is a matrix with one row per term:




Estimate
Std. Error
t value
Pr(>|t|)




(Intercept)
…
…
…
…


X
…
…
…
← p-value




Once we have simulated X and Y, we fit a simple linear regression of Y on X. The p-value for the slope coefficient tests whether X has a significant effect on Y. We extract it from the coefficients matrix returned by summary(lm()). This is the core operation we will repeat thousands of times to build the empirical p-value distribution.

title: Lecture 4: Multiple Testing Correction
text:
Empirical Distribution of P-values
Repeat the regression 10,000 times, save the p-values, plot the histogram.

https://bios25328.hakyimlab.org/post/2022/04/06/multiple-testing-2022/

By repeating the procedure thousands of times, we can empirically estimate the distribution of p-values under the null and alternative. This makes abstract statistical theory concrete.

title: Lecture 4: Multiple Testing Correction
text:
Simulated Null Distribution of P-values


The distribution of p-values under the null is approximately uniform — exactly as the theoretical definition of a p-value predicts. Each bin of the histogram contains roughly the same number of p-values.

title: Lecture 4: Multiple Testing Correction
text:
Simulated Null Distribution of P-values (zoom)


Zooming in on the null p-value distribution confirms the uniform shape. The horizontal line shows the expected frequency per bin (1/number of bins). Deviations from this flat line are random variation due to the finite number of simulations.

title: Lecture 4: Multiple Testing Correction
section: Histogram of P-values: Mix of Null and Alternative
text:
Histogram of P-values: Mix of Null and Alternative


When we mix 80% null and 20% alternative phenotypes, the histogram is no longer flat. There is a large spike near 0 — these are the true discoveries. The rest of the distribution (away from 0) stays roughly uniform, as expected under the null.

title: Lecture 4: Multiple Testing Correction
section: What Does the Mix Tell Us?
text:
What Does the Mix Tell Us?


Under the null alone, with 10,000 tests at α = 0.05, we expect 500 significant results by chance. When we observe 2,236 significant results, the extra ~1,736 likely come from the alternative distribution. This motivates a data-driven estimate of the false discovery rate.

title: Lecture 4: Multiple Testing Correction
text:
Two Frameworks




Called Significant
Called not significant
Total




Null true
F
\(m_0 - F\)
\(m_0\)


Alt true
T
\(m_1 - T\)
\(m_1\)


Total
S
\(m - S\)
\(m\)





Bonferroni correction
Controls Family-Wise Error Rate (FWER):
\[P(F \geq 1) < \alpha\]
Achieved by requiring \(p < \alpha / \text{# tests}\)

False Discovery Rate (FDR)
\[\text{FDR} = E\left(\frac{F}{S}\right)\]
q-value: minimum FDR attainable when a feature is called significant


Bonferroni controls the probability of making any false positive — very strict. FDR controls the proportion of false positives among all significant results. FDR is more lenient and better powered when many true signals exist, which is common in GWAS.

title: Lecture 4: Multiple Testing Correction
text:
Calculating FDR
Under the null, we expected 500 significant results by chance but got 2,236.
\[\widehat{\text{TDR}} = \frac{\text{observed significant} - \text{expected under null}}{\text{observed significant}} = \frac{2236 - 500}{2236} \approx 0.78\]
\[\widehat{\text{FDR}} = 1 - \widehat{\text{TDR}} \approx 0.18\]

The False Discovery Rate is the proportion of significant results that are actually null. Here, about 18% of the 2,236 significant tests are expected to be false positives. In practice, we use the Storey & Tibshirani (2003) q-value framework to estimate this more precisely.

title: Lecture 4: Multiple Testing Correction
text:
Simulation Results Table




Called Significant
Called not significant




Null true
411
7545


Alt true
1825
219



Q: What are the false positive rate and power in this simulation?

411 false positives out of 7,956 null hypotheses gives a false positive rate of ~5%. 1,825 true positives out of 2,044 alternative hypotheses gives power of ~89%.
The observed FDR = 411 / (411 + 1825) = 411 / 2236 ≈ 18%, consistent with our estimate.

title: Lecture 4: Multiple Testing Correction
text:
Homework Problems

What is the proportion of false discoveries if we use a significance level of 0.01?
What is the proportion of false discoveries if we use Bonferroni correction as the significance level?
What is the proportion of missed signals (proportion of true associations with p-values greater than the Bonferroni threshold)?


These homework problems build intuition about the trade-off between false positives (false discovery rate) and false negatives (missed signals, i.e., 1 − power) as the significance threshold becomes more or less stringent.

title: Lecture 4: Multiple Testing Correction
section: Q-values Are Small When the Alternative is True
text:
Q-values Are Small When the Alternative is True


The q-value is the minimum FDR you accept when calling a feature significant. Associations with small q-values (near 0) are likely true positives. Associations with large q-values (near 1) are likely null. The q-value package (Storey & Tibshirani 2003) estimates the proportion of null hypotheses π₀ from the data and uses it to calibrate FDR estimates.

title: Lecture 4: Multiple Testing Correction
text:
π₀ and π₁ = 1 − π₀

π₀: estimated proportion of features under the null
π₁ = 1 − π₀: estimated proportion of features under the alternative

When all tests are null: \(\pi_0 \approx 1\)
When 80% are null: \(\pi_0 \approx 0.8\)

π₀ is estimated from the right tail of the p-value distribution, where we expect only null p-values. A high π₀ means most hypotheses are null; a lower π₀ means many true signals are present. This estimate feeds directly into the FDR calculation.

title: Lecture 4: Multiple Testing Correction
text:
Summary









Method
Controls
Stringency
Best for




Bonferroni
FWER: P(any false positive)
Very strict
Few tests, high cost of any error


FDR / q-value
E(false positives / significant)
Moderate
Many tests, many true signals



GWAS significance threshold: 5 × 10⁻⁸ (Bonferroni for ~1M independent tests)

The choice between Bonferroni and FDR depends on context. In GWAS, the 5 × 10⁻⁸ threshold is a community convention based on Bonferroni. In downstream analyses (e.g., eQTL studies, pathway enrichment), FDR-based thresholds are more common because many true signals are expected.

title: Lecture 4: Multiple Testing Correction
text:
References

Storey, J. D., and Tibshirani, R. (2003). Statistical significance for genomewide studies. PNAS 100(16): 9440–45.
Benjamini, Y., and Hochberg, Y. (1995). Controlling the false discovery rate: a practical and powerful approach to multiple testing. JRSS-B 57(1): 289–300.
Risch, N., and Merikangas, K. (1996). The future of genetic studies of complex human diseases. Science 273(5281): 1516–17.
